chunks.text: Yeah, you just want to Right. The meeting is resumed. That's 1,000 if it ends.

So we're hearing evidence in the Fisheries Amendment Bill. We've got 12. First up, we have Steve Abel of the Green Party of Aotearoa New Zealand. You've got- Five 10 minutes. How you use that time is up to you. Suggest you leave some time for questions at the end.

Hard questions. So away you go. Tēnā koutou katoa. Thank you very much for the opportunity to present today. I'm speaking on behalf of Green co-leader Chlöe Swarbrick, who could not be with us this morning. The Green Party are here today to emphasize to the committee the public outrage at this Fisheries Amendment Bill.

While New Zealanders are demanding relief from a cost of living crisis, this government has chosen to introduce a bill that fundamentally alters the purpose and direction of fisheries management in Aotearoa. At its heart, this bill is about who this government works for. And on that question, the answer is very clear.

This is a government that has not yet met a part of the environment it won't try to open up, extract from, and convert into short-term profit, regardless of the long-term consequences and regardless of who pays the price. Because the reality is the price is not paid by industry. The price is paid by everyday New Zealanders and by the environment.

It is paid by people who just want to go fishing with their whanau. It is paid by our communities who rely on healthy ecosystems, and it is paid by future generations who inherit what we leave behind. Here in Aotearoa, we pride ourselves on a simple idea that you can go out and catch a feed for your family.

That access to a healthy, thriving ocean is part of what makes this place special. This bill undermines that very birthright. It treats fisheries not as something we all share, but as something to be maximized, traded, and exported. And that brings me to a second point.

This is about public resources. Our oceans and the species that live therein are being handed over to private interests. But this bill continues a pattern from this government that is shifting control away from the public and towards a small number of well-connected commercial players.

It entrenches the power of quota holders. It reduces the obligation to provide for the public before allocating to industry. And it pushes everyday New Zealanders closer to the margins of a system that they should be at the center of. This is not an accident. This is a political choice.

And it raises a simple question: Who is the system being redesigned for? Because it is not being redesigned for the public. Overwhelmingly, public opposition has made that clear. We already know that more than 27,000 people submitted on earlier proposals, with over 90% opposition.

Nine out of 10 submitters said no. And yet here we are. This bill is being brought forward anyway, rushed with only some parts walked back after public backlash. That is not good process. That is not democratic accountability. That is a government deciding it knows better than the people it represents.

We've already seen one example of this, the proposal to remove minimum size limits for commercial fishers. Only after immense public pressure did the minister back down, and we're yet to see that manifest in the actual legislation. But New Zealanders need to understand that this was just one part of a terrible bill. Because the rest of the bill still weakens oversight, reduces transparency, and tilts the playing field towards industry. This is about weakening accountability while protecting industry. We know there are serious issues in the fishing sector.

When cameras were introduced onboard vessels, they revealed what had long been suspected, underreporting bycatch and waste is at much higher levels than previously thought. That transparency was a step forward. But now, instead of building on that progress, this bill goes backwards. It limits public access to that footage, it restricts scrutiny, and it introduces fines of up to $50,000 for sharing that information.

Now we need to think about that. They are proposing harsher penalties for exposing potential wrongdoing than many of the penalties faced for the wrongdoing itself. That is a political signal about whose interests are being protected, and it is not the public interest. Because the direction of travel has been clear from the beginning.

Corporate interests over long-term sustainability. This bill poses a question about the kind of country we want to be. Do we want to be a country where our ocean and nature itself are treated as short-term commodities? Or do we want to be a country that protects and values the natural world so that everyone can share in it now and in the future?

Do we want a system designed around export growth at any cost? This bill is a clear statement from this government that industry interests come first. Public access comes second. Environmental protections are negotiable. And overwhelming public opposition can be ignored.Green Party fervently opposes this legislation.

We urge the committee to reject this bill, but more than that, we'd only want to defend the status quo. We can and should go further. We need to ban bottom trawling and protect at least 30% of our oceans, because there is no separating the economy from the environment that sustains all of us.

Because New Zealanders deserve better than a system that sells off our environment, weakens accountability, and sidelines the public voice. We can have fisheries that are sustainable, transparent, and fair, but this bill is not the way to get there. Thank you. Thank you, Steve.
Any questions? Doesn't seem so. Thank you very much. Thank you very much.
We'll move on. Is the Parliamentary Commissioner team here? Oh, excellent.

So next up, we have the Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment. So we've got, I assume it's Rebecca here in person, and you have some online as well. Is that correct? Mr. Chair, they should be here in person. Okay. I had the benefit of just being next door, so I was able to just walk straight through. But I'm sure they'll be here in due course.

Okay. Wouldn't want to hold the committee business up. So you have 20 minutes. Kia ora. Thank you. So, fire away. Look, members, Mr. Chair, the commissioner is currently on leave and cannot attend committee today. I'm sorry. However, this oral submission has been signed off from him.

I'm Rebecca Thornley, the commissioner's general counsel, and hopefully will be joined by several colleagues shortly. This submission is made in the context of the PCE's functions under Section 16 of the Environment Act, undertaken with a view to maintain or improve the environment, spanning the entire environmental management system of which fisheries management is a part.

The PCE submission today will address three aspects that were not covered in the written submission. These are the impact on fisheries decisions, including standard factors, application of the Official Information Act, and access to onboard camera footage, and the interface between the Fisheries Act and the Resource Management Act, which at the moment may be replaced by the Planning Bill and Natural Environment Bill.

Turning to the first point of decision making. This bill substantially changes the way that the minister will make total allowable catch decisions. While some of these changes are discussed in the written submission, I address the introduction of standard factors as decision-making criteria.

Standard factors limit the extent to which the minister can take into account environmental principles when setting the TAC, which otherwise apply to all persons exercising functions and powers under the Act. This was part of the fundamental design of the 1996 Act.

These principles and the philosophy that sustainability underpins TAC decisions are consistent with New Zealand's international obligations under UNCLOS, as originally endorsed by this very committee in its final report on the Fisheries Bill in 1996.

Standard factors include the environmental conditions that affect specified stock and the social, cultural, and economic factors the minister considers relevant. By limiting the application of the environmental principles, not prioritizing environmental factors over this list of other matters, and the reference to TAC being removed from sustainability measures, TAC decisions will fail to uphold the fundamental purpose of fisheries management.

I'd like to introduce my colleague, Geoff Simmons, our Chief Advisor, Strategy and Policy. Kia ora. Just in time. Over the long term, setting TAC should be entirely a matter of responding to biophysical realities. Imposing concepts like quota management areas and maximum sustainable yield for single stocks onto complex and mobile biological systems isn't straightforward. As EDS points out, in the absence of other tools addressing localized biophysical issues will sometimes require maintaining or building a greater abundance of stock than maximum sustainable yield

would suggest. Social, cultural, and economic factors don't alter that biophysical reality. If adjustments in TAC are needed, it may be possible to do that in a way that considers social, cultural, and economic realities in the short term. However, in the long term, consideration of social, cultural, and economic factors will detract from the necessary focus on biophysical realities to the detriment of both the marine environment and fisheries.

We acknowledge that the TAC may not always be the ideal place to embed the consideration of all environmental impacts of fishing.Ideally, there would be greater use of spatial planning, particularly different kinds of marine reserves, and potentially some maximum size limits for catch in certain areas.

Managing fisheries solely through TAC decisions is really our second-best policy response. However, in lieu of other spatial protection tools, the TAC is all that we have. Mm. And our recommendations on this issue are to not exclude TAC from sustainability measures, do not limit the consideration of environmental principles to the standard factors, and make explicit that environmental conditions affecting stock is the primary consideration, but the minister may have regard to social, cultural, and economic factors.

Mr. Chair, moving to the application of the OIA and access to onboard camera footage. The bill proposes to exclude application of the OIA to camera recordings and instead provides limited circumstances in which disclosure may occur. The policy rationale does not establish the risk that this amendment seeks to address based on the number of OIA requests that have been made and the volume of camera footage that has been disclosed since the requirement was introduced. As we understand it, as at 12 March 2025, MPI had received

14 OIA requests, but we are not aware of any footage having been released so far. The policy problem, which is outlined in appendix four of the regulatory impact statement, is that privacy and commercially sensitive information may be disclosed through an OIA release.

However, the OIA has always provided for the withholding of information on a number of grounds, including the protection of privacy. It appears, Mr. Chair, that there is no convincing evidence to explain why these existing protections are inadequate.

The transparency that access to camera footage brings with it incentivizes operators to comply with catch management requirements. It ensures accuracy in reporting requirements, and as the ombudsman has submitted, it upholds the purposes of both the OIA and the Fisheries Act.

Mr. Chair, I also provide a comparison to that even heavily protected national security information where the need for secrecy is so well justified and understood. This remains subject to oversight and public assurance through the Inspector General of Intelligence and Security.

The PCE recommends that the OIA should continue to apply to onboard footage. But if the committee is seeking assurances relating to disclosure, we note that the ombudsman has suggested an amendment to the OIA to that effect and is prepared to issue guidance to assist.

The PCE supports the submission of the ombudsman, which the committee should carefully consider. Finally, Mr. Chair, turning to the interface between the Fisheries Act and RMA reform. Through the decision of the Court of Appeal in the case of Mōtiti, it was confirmed that the RMA had a wider focus than the Fisheries Act, protecting biodiversity and natural character values in the coastal marine area for their own sake rather than for their connection to the utilization of fisheries resource.

This decision provided councils with clear criteria to apply when they are developing rules which would impact fisheries management. However, this was responded to by the Resource Management Consenting and Other System Changes Amendment Act 2025, which constrained the scope of the rules that can be imposed by regional councils.

In doing so, it muddied the clarity that was provided by the Court of Appeal. It introduced a process which confuses the performance of functions under the two acts. For example, despite being prohibited by RMA Section 30 subsection 2 from undertaking functions regarding fisheries management, the process requires council to comprehensively assess the impacts on fishing, despite regional councils not being fisheries experts.

The concurrence of the Director General of MPI on proposed rules is also required, though their decision to concur is limited in its scope and does not include consideration of the purpose or the principles of the Fisheries Act. These changes to the RMA are carried over into the Planning Bill and Natural Environment Bill, which are currently before another select committee.

This bill seeks to weaken environmental considerations that fisheries decisions should be having regard to. The Fisheries Act, if amended, would no longer provide assurance that fisheries management will be grounded in the biophysical constraints of the marine environment.

Weakening environmental considerations under both legislative schemes that are supposed to protect marine biodiversity means considerable riskFor degradation of the marine environment and fisheries. Mr. Chair, that concludes the prepared comments from the PCE, and we're happy to take questions from members.

Very good. Thank you. We've got a question from Rachel first. Okay. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you for your submission. I'm keen to ask for your thoughts on part of the bill that you haven't actually commented on. So I'm hoping you have considered this, and that's the part around the carry forward of ACE. So, as you'll be aware, there's an annual catch entitlement, and there are some species that can currently have up to 10% carry forward. And what the bill would allow is, in extreme circumstances, a 100% carry forward if the

minister made that determination. There have been some concerns raised about that, which I share, around that could have an impact on the sustainability of a stock. So I'm just interested in whether you've considered that particular part of the bill, any suggestions you might have to improve it, any concerns you might have around the sustainability measures.

We haven't looked at that one, unfortunately. No, not in any great detail. Okay. Yeah. Okay. We're more than happy to receive questions, Mr. Chair, from members if you would like written feedback from the PCE on this. I'd really appreciate that one. So the particular issue, there's some really good examples where it has been used and then hasn't been used.

So rock lobster gives some good examples. During COVID, there was an order in council so that they could transfer quota after a market collapse, and then they considered it after Cyclone Gabrielle and decided not to do a carryover. The concern that's been raised is at the moment it's 10%, this would allow 100%, and is there enough information on the stock, all of those things.

So yeah, if you could provide us with some written advice on that particular issue, I'd appreciate that. Yeah. I think what we could do afterwards is get the- Some written questions... written questions. Yeah. Yeah. Steve, you had a question. Yeah. Thank you very much for the submission. In your decision making under uncertainty, you point out the effective assessment requires biological and ecological information, catch composition, size, age structure, to estimate biomass, population dynamics, all of these factors.

And yet, if I understood correctly, you just articulated that you did support the minister taking into consideration environmental, social, and cultural factors as well as the biological. My concern in that is what are, particularly the economic, social, or cultural factors.

Culture is in a different category, I think, but what are the economic or social factors that would override a biological need to reduce the catch? Yeah. As I indicated, we see that as a short term consideration, and I think the current drafting of the bill does allow for those considerations to be taken into the decision making process when thinking about a transition. So if reductions have to be made, how is that managed from an economic and a social perspective?

And I think the current system is working well in that respect. So I think that in the short term, those sorts of factors can be taken into consideration, but in the long term, it's inappropriate. As a supplement. Yeah. It seems to me a concern, and it seems to me a weakening of the basic evaluation of the ecological health of the stock to allow the minister to consider economic things, unless that's within a very narrow scope of something like what you just described.

So would you want to see greater clarification of the extent to which those factors could be considered, and in such a way as they don't override the ecological and biological reality of the health of the ecosystem and the stock? Mm-hmm. Yeah. Yes, and I think that the current drafting of the act allows for that.

Oh. Yeah. Right. Rachel. Just to sup on that, because this has been a topic of discussion amongst submitters around the factors that we've, I call them. I guess this is something you might need to come back to us again as a written question. Would you consider, I guess, a hierarchy of the factors in terms of the importance of which ones are considered?

Because I think that's one of the things. What's the hierarchy and the order that you would consider them in terms of which ones carry more weight and which ones don't? That is certainly a drafting solution that the committee could pursue- Mm... and request further advice from officials about what that might look like if that was what you were minded to do. We're having very similar conversations at the moment to the environment committee in relation to the planning and natural environment bills in terms of the environment, and in this case, the marine environment

will tell you what the biophysical constraints are. It doesn't matter what decisions are made. It doesn't matter what hierarchy of considerations you try to apply through the law. The environment will tell you what is or is not possible. There will or will not be fish to be fished. So on that basis, the submission of the commissioner has been that your biophysical constraints must be your primary consideration, not because this house says that that's what is required. That is simply the environment in which you are

operating, and that will tell you what is required. Rachel. I do have a sup on that. Sorry. Which I guess one of the challenges in the ocean, and againHappy for you to come back. The challenges in the ocean is that we can't see all of it, right? And so unlike making land-based assessments, in terms of the biological impacts of the ocean, the ability to see everything that's out there is so limited, both in terms of fish stock, but then other parts of ocean health.

So, have you got any thoughts about investment that's needed to actually get that level of information so that we can make the decision about the impact on the environment? So these are questions that we would welcome certainly following this submission today. Unfortunately, our chief scientist is also currently on leave, but both he and the commissioner are returning next week and would be the best placed expert to assist with providing information to the committee on those matters.

But I do draw your attention to our written submission in the section Decision Making Under Uncertainty, where we do discuss these sorts of issues and talk about how a more precautionary approach might work in that stocks that have good information have more flexibility than stocks with poorer information.

And those are the sorts of incentives that could be built into the system. Yeah. Steve, you're this up. Yeah. I guess the concern is when you say the biophysical limits of the ocean will be self-evident, there will or there will not be fish. I guess what we're trying to do is manage so that there are fish because clearly when there has been evidence that a fishery is in trouble, there hasn't been sufficient rapidity of intervention to protect the remaining stock so that it can recover more quickly. The Hawke fishery being a recent example, I

guess. And our concern, just to belabor this point somewhat, is that if we give scope for other considerations than simply the available evidence on the biological and the ecological health of the fishery, then that will tend to want to override the evidence that the fishery can't handle the current take.

Again, as Geoff responded, we'd point you to the relevant part of our written submission, but we'd be certainly- If you don't disagree with that- We'd be happy to provide more information if you had a further question or statement you wanted to put to the commissioner, Geoff. Yeah. What I would say at a high level is that the design of the QMS was intended to create incentives for self-regulation.

And I think the fact is that that's worked in some fisheries and not others, and that requires a much deeper analysis on the interplay between the different players in the fisheries system. I think the answer to your question would be it depends on the fishery.

Yeah. And a lot more analysis would be required to really get into that. Yeah. Susan. You said it that towards the end of your submission, it seems perverse to knowingly undermine access to information about our fish stocks. I just wondered what you meant by that because I would've thought that actually we've got better information about our fish stocks now than we had sort of 20 or 30 years ago or even before and it seems to be improving.

But just in particular what you meant by it seems perverse to knowingly undermine access to information about our fish stocks. We draw your attention to the paragraph under Decision Making Under Uncertainty, which catalogs that there has been, in recent years, a fall in the overall funding for research both from the fisheries sector and the Crown.

So that's what we are referring to there. Okay. So talking about the... Sorry, you're talking about the amount of research, the funding of research- That's right... specifically? Yeah. Okay. I'll give my burning question to my colleague- So just-... Susan. Yeah. Just- Last question burning question is you talked about fish stocks as being the kind of primary consideration, but would you agree that actually ocean health as well matters and that there needs to, that it's not just about fish stock, but actually the importance of the role the ocean

plays, yeah, is an important consideration. Again, I draw your attention to our written submission, in the last section on failure to move towards an integrated approach to fisheries management. Some of the criticisms that exist about the QMS currently are that it takes a single species approach.

And what the commissioner is saying here is that really these changes are doubling down on that. We're moving even more to a more single species approach than previously. So yes, of course, we would advocate for a system which takes a broader perspective, but as I said previously, the tools that exist within the system are not ideal for that. And we have spoken previously in other submissions about the need for marine

spatial planning. Yeah. And that that sort of approach would give you more tools to deal with ecosystem impacts, rather than trying to do everything through setting tac. Yeah. Thank you very much. We've come to time. And I'm sure we'll have you back in at some stage in the future for various issues, so.

Great. And yeah, do get in touch with written questions- Yeah... we'll get to. We'll probably put together some question far through for post-hearing inquiry. Thank you. Got them. Thank you very much.

Right. Next we've got a change to the agenda. We were due to have Gisborne Fisheries- Yeah, what happened to Selvadurai? Selvadurai. He's on a plane. Yeah. So- Delayed. I don't know. He said he couldn't make it. He's on a plane ... we've got Deep Sea Conservation Coalition instead.

So they're going to be by Zoom or- Online ... they're online now. Good morning. Welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee. We have Duncan Currie, who's the legal advisor for Deep Sea Conservation Coalition online. You have 10 minutes. How you use that time's up to you.

So, fire away. Thank you, Chair, and good morning members of the committee. Tena koutou. Thank you for the opportunity to speak today. Our interest in this bill is simple. We are seeking stronger protection for the ocean, particularly from destructive fishing methods such as bottom trawling on seamounts and similar deep sea features.

This bill takes New Zealand's fisheries management in the opposite direction. Our recommendation is that the Fisheries Amendment Bill be withdrawn in its entirety. We do not say this because fisheries management is perfect, it is not. Reform is needed, but this bill is not the reform that New Zealand needs.

It weakens environmental safeguards, reduces transparency, shuts non-industry voices out of decision-making, and makes it harder for unlawful decisions to be challenged. I will focus on five core concerns. Firstly, that the bill removes fundamental environmental guardrails from fisheries management.

The Fisheries Act currently contains clear environmental principles. Those principles require decision-makers to maintain associated and dependent species above a level that ensures their long-term viability, that's Section 9A, maintain biological diversity in Section 9B, and protect habitats of particular significance for fisheries management in Section 9C.

These are the foundation of sustainable fisheries management. They're not optional extras. But the proposed Clause 13F would allow the minister, in setting catch limits, to take into account adverse environmental effects and environmental principles only to the extent they are relevant to narrower standard factors.

In practical terms, this could mean the minister could treat a fishery in isolation rather than part of a wide ocean ecosystem. It risks taking us backwards towards a narrow single species approach. This is inconsistent with science, it is bad policy, and it is inconsistent with New Zealand's international obligations.

Under the Law of the Sea Convention, New Zealand must take into account the effects of fishing on species associated with or dependent upon harvested species. And under the UN Fish Stocks Agreement, New Zealand must assess impacts on target stocks, associated independent species, and the wider ecosystem and apply the precautionary approach.

Clause 13F risks New Zealand routinely failing against these obligations. Secondly, the bill shuts stakeholders and the public out of decision-making. The bill would allow the minister to set TACs for multiple years, with quotas rolling over automatically.

Once that multi-year decision is made, there may be no annual opportunity for stakeholders to scrutinize the settings, respond to new information, or raise concerns. This is deeply concerning. Fish populations can decline, ecosystems can change, climate impacts can intensify, new bycatch information can emerge, and new habitat impacts can be withdrawn. Yet under this bill, decisions could effectively continue on autopilot.

This bill also allows non-regulatory measures to proceed without consultation for any reason. This is an extraordinarily broad discretion and could apply, for example, to voluntary measures proposed by industry. This would shut out public tangata whenua, environmental organizations, rec fishers, and other stakeholders out of decisions affecting the health of the ocean.

Third, the bill erodes transparency by burying onboard camera footage. You've heard a great deal about this. Cameras help build trust, but only if the information they gather can be meaningfully scrutinized. In effect, footage of what happens on fishing vessels may never see the light of day.

Privacy concerns can be addressed through proportionate tools such as blurring faces, releasing still images, releasing short segments, redacting sensitive material, or providing summaries where necessary. The maximum fine for releasing camera footage also creates an absurd imbalance. You've heard a great deal about this.

Fourth, the bill risks incentivizing less selective and more destructive fishing. We're particularly concerned about the changes to deemed values and discarding rules. Deemed values are supposed to create a financial disincentive for catching fish above quota or catching species a fisher does not have quota for.

But the bill would allow a lower alternative deemed value for non-targeted fish immediately frozen at sea. The current minister has already indicated an intention to use this for deep water freezer vessels.This would create a perverse incentive for industrial offshore fishing methods, including bottom trawling, over more selective and low-impact methods.

Fifth, the bill sets an unworkable timeframe for judicial review. The proposed Clause 313A would restrict the timeframe for filing judicial review proceedings to 20 working days. This is simply unrealistic and insulates decision-making from lawful scrutiny.

So what should be done? We agree that fisheries reform is needed, but it should be a reform that starts from the need for a healthy ocean, because healthy fisheries depend on healthy ecosystems. More than 100,000 people have supported calls to end bottom trawling on seamounts and similar deep sea features, and more than 73,000 people recently supported Live Ocean's call to action to phase out bottom trawling, including the immediate protection of seamounts.

This is a public mandate for stronger ocean protection. This is not a public mandate for weakening environmental principles, reducing transparency, limiting consultation, and restricting access to judicial review. In closing, we recommend that the committee recommends this entire bill be withdrawn, and instead the government begins a genuine review of fisheries management, one which does not take this bill as its starting point, and one that asks how New Zealand can move towards selective, sustainable, low-impact fishing while protecting taonga species, critical

features like seamounts and other vulnerable marine ecosystems. The review should consider the work already being done through the EEZ Benthic Forum and the recommendations made by environmental NGOs to protect seamounts. This bill weakens the environmental principles of the Fisheries Act, gives too much discretion and power to ministers, reduces transparency and public participation, and hides information from scrutiny. It makes it harder to challenge unlawful decisions.

In closing, New Zealand can have fisheries that provides food and livelihoods, and you've heard this today. A prosperity of fisheries begins with the protection of the ocean they depend upon. This bill will not achieve that. For these reasons and others, in our written submission, the Deep Sea Conservation Coalition urges the committee to recommend that the Fisheries Amendment Bill be withdrawn in its entirety. Ngā mihi.

Thank you very much, Duncan. Now we're going to a question from Steve Abel. Yeah. Thank you, Duncan, for such a substantive and clear submission. Can you just expand, for the benefit of the committee, on the Clause 40 deemed value stuff and how that could incentivize more bottom trawling over more sustainable fishing methods and the perverse incentive it creates?

Yeah, that's right. Well, it's really getting it the other way around because deemed values are supposed to, as I said, create a financial disincentive for catching fish above quota or catching species that a fisher does not have quota for. Yeah. But the bill would allow a lower alternative deemed value to be set for non-targeted fish immediately frozen at sea.

And that's where the perverse incentive would be. It would move the incentive towards offshore fishing methods and industrial fishing over more selective and lower impact methods. And we believe that New Zealand should be working towards more selective, low impact, more sustainable fishing rather than create these sorts of loopholes that reward indiscriminate fishing methods.

One for another. Yeah. Just to sup on that, if that's all right. Do you not think, in that case, with the deemed values, if that goes against the fisheries quota, so it's counted as their quota, then that would not be an incentive for fishing methods and targeted species to be more specific?

Not if that's really clear. Well, no, because they're going to be lower deemed values, and therefore, that's really the inherent disincentive there, as I see it. Steve, you had another question. Well, I was just going to ask about the carryover A stuff, catch entitlements.

You flagging that as a challenge, but the conversation that's been going on is about the reasonable expectation that if a fisher is not taking their quota one year, that they should have some ability to take a bit more the next, but you think that's a flawed idea? We do, because you need to have the assessment year by year.

That's our point, is that the ocean changes, fisheries change. We mentioned climate change. The stocks can collapse or move. We've seen all of these happening in real time in the orange roughy fishery, for example. And so by setting multi-year TACs, you're simply putting the fisheries management on autopilot, and you don't have the annual scrutiny to ensure that your fisheries allocation is going to be sustainable. We think it's very dangerous.

Yeah. Just a quick side, because multi-year TACs and carryover are two different mechanisms in the bill. Correct. And so the carryover one, a question I've asked some who have raised the concerns, which I share, is if we do actually have evidence that there isn't going to be an impact on the sustainability of the stock, would you support it in those circumstances?

I think the carryover provisions are like multi-year TACs in the sense that, again, they increase the pressure on the fish stocks and also non-fisheries impacts such as impacts on vulnerable marine ecosystems. That's, I think, one of our core contingent or core problems we have with carryover is that there's really very little evidence that they'll do anything other than increase the intensity of fishing pressure on those fish stocks and on associated independent species such as bycatch and VMEs. I think that's really the

core of our concern, Ms. Boyack. Thank you. Thank you very much, Duncan. We've come to time. We appreciate you taking the time to submit to the committee. Thanks, Duncan. Excellent. Thank you, committee. Kia ora. Thank you. Now we're moving on to the Royal Forest and Bird Society, and they're going to be via Zoom as well.

Good morning. Welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee. And you have 10 minutes for your submission. How you use your time's up to you, and I'll leave it with you. Thank you, Chair. Tēnā koutou katoa. Ko Erika Tongen toku ingoa. I am the acting Chief Executive of Forest and Bird, and joining me today, also on Zoom, are my colleagues Richard Kepi and May Downing.

So, Forest and Bird is Aotearoa's biggest environmental charity. We work across all environmental domains, including the oceans from coast to deep sea. We speak today on behalf of our 100,000 supporters who live, work, and volunteer in communities across the country and who are drawn from all political walks of life.

Forest and Bird has participated in various legislative processes culminating in marine protected areas from the Pollen Island Marine Reserve to the newly protected areas in the Hauraki Gulf. We've also fought hard in the courts on fisheries right up to the Supreme Court, where we successfully argued that when determining stock recovery periods for tarakihi, the rebuild period is based on biological and environmental conditions for the stock, and that it excludes social, cultural, and economic considerations.

Ms. Downing will address you a bit further on that today. With New Zealand having the fourth largest EEZ in the world and being a renowned marine biodiversity hotspot, it is absolutely imperative we get the legal framework for fisheries management and sustainability right. Like the vast majority of submitters on this bill and people up and down the country, we feel the bill is fundamentally flawed and it should be withdrawn.

It will lead to uncertainty and environmental degradation. The alleged benefits will only accrue to a small number of quota holders and only in the short term, to the detriment of both the environment but also the wider fishing stakeholders, including the fishers themselves in the long term.

This bill is not about sustainability. It would lead to pillaging of our oceans with a long-term cost. We say the bill should be withdrawn in full, and officials should be directed back to the drawing board to consider an ecosystem-based fisheries management approach. Our written submission sets out our concerns in more detail, but in the event that the bill is retained, my colleague May will now draw your attention to some of the most egregious proposed amendments.

Over to you, May. Thanks, Erika. Tēnā koutou. My name is May Downing. I am the acting General Counsel at Forest and Bird. As Erika mentioned, our view is that the bill is so flawed that it should be thrown out completely. However, the government did wish to try and resuscitate this legislation.

I'd just like to draw the committee to a few key points that we find particularly troubling. Firstly, the ability to consider social, cultural, and economic factors when determining rebuild periods needs to be removed. The proposed amendments in Clause 10 of the bill seek to overturn the Supreme Court decision on tarakihi, and we say they are inconsistent with the statutory purpose of the act.

Under Section 13 ( 2 ) of the Fisheries Act, the minister is required to set a TAC for a stock at a level that will enable the stock's recovery within a period appropriate to the stock and any environmental conditions affecting that stock. Under Section 13 ( 3 ) , the minister is to have regard to relevant social, cultural, and economic factors when considering the way in which and rate at which a stock is moved towards or above a level that can produce the maximum sustainable yield.

Now, the Supreme Court in the tarakihi decision confirmed that the minister must assess the period appropriate to the stock by reference to the biological characteristics and environmental conditions, which I understand other submitters have referred to as the biophysical limits, and without regard to social, cultural, and economic factors.The court also found that where the stock's biological characteristics and environmental conditions allow for more than one appropriate recovery period, that's where the social, cultural, and economic factors can then

be engaged. That's where that can be referred to in selecting an alternative or the appropriate rebuild period. Now, the bill replaces this approach with the requirement to only consider the scientifically appropriate period as one of many competing factors, including social and economic factors that the minister considers relevant.

If rebuild periods are set by reference to social and economic factors, the fishing industry is then able to influence the rebuild period and make it entirely lawful to set longer and more unsustainable rebuild periods, and longer rebuild periods allow higher total allowable catches.

The outcome is a fish stock that is held at a very low level for longer, making the stock more vulnerable to sharp decline if unpredictable environmental changes are experienced, and this reduces the species' ability to have a role in the wider ecosystem. For these reasons, scientific and environmental considerations should override other factors when determining the rebuild period.

Such considerations are fundamentally about the health and sustainability of the fishery, the wider ecosystem, and basically ensuring future generations can also fish. If the bill is not thrown out, we seek that sections 13B, clauses four, five, and six, and we can provide these to the committee afterwards, are removed.

And we also seek that sections 13 ( 2 ) and 13 ( 3 ) of the act are left alone. Ultimately, you can't enjoy the social, cultural, and economic benefits of a thriving fishery without the fish in the first place. The next point is about the minimum size limits in the fisheries regulations.

We understand the government was going to drop this proposed amendment, which is positive, but it's still within the bill, and we note that this is one of many things that should be dropped. The bill proposes to amend the fisheries commercial fishing regulations to remove minimum size limits.

Allowing the take of undersized fish disrupts natural life cycles, reduces future breeding capacity, and risks the total collapse of fish stocks. Length limits should be retained to discourage their take. The ability to carry over ACE under proposed new section 67C and 67D should also be removed.

Saving uncaught annual catch entitlement and carrying it forward onto other fishing years means large amounts of uncaught catch can accumulate. This could lead to excessive fishing pressure in a single season and decrease sustainability by potentially causing a catastrophic decline in fish stocks, which are unable to recover quickly enough.

If a higher than expected catch happens due to carry forward, it can disrupt the ecosystem and food web and affect predator-prey relationships. I now move on to the provisions on camera recordings. We say that subpart five, which deals with camera recordings, should be removed, in particular section 227H, which prevents the OIA from applying to camera recordings. This provision is essentially using a sledgehammer to crack a nut.

There's been a modest amount of OIA requests for recordings. As of April 2025, 10 of 15 have been fully withheld on the basis of commercial sensitivity or protecting the maintenance of the law. There have been no complaints upheld by the ombudsman that information was improperly released in breach of requirements of commercial sensitivity or privacy, and we say that there are already sufficient safeguards in existing legislation to protect these things.

Exempting camera footage from the OIA is also not a legitimate public policy approach and significantly undermines public confidence and transparency in the fishing industry. And the last point I draw your attention to is the time limit on filing judicial reviews.

Judicial reviews are a critical mechanism for upholding the rule of law. They enable the courts to scrutinize decisions and actions of public bodies, ensuring they are lawful, fair, and reasonable, and decision-makers should be held accountable to retain public trust. A time limit is also ill-suited to the Fisheries Act, where sufficient time is often required to gather and obtain large amounts of technical information before deciding on whether a claim is even necessary.

It may risk parties bringing claims when they're not required for the sake of getting them filed on time. Thank you. I'll now hand back to Erica or Richard. Right. I think we've actually run out of time. Is that correct, Chair? You got 30 seconds.

Okay. Well, perhaps I'll pass over to questions then. Okay. Question. That was great, though. Thank you. GreatStephen. Just a very quick one. Explain to us why you are particularly against the inclusion of economic, social, and cultural considerations. Mae, would you like to take that one?

I can, and someone can jump in if I haven't quite captured it. But in a nutshell, it's when setting that rebuild period, the first thing that we need to get right is ensuring that the biophysical limits are properly set. It's only after that, if those biophysical limits provide for an array of different recovery periods which are ecologically safe, for want of a better description, we say that's when it's appropriate for economic factors to be considered.

If there's a choice that means that the rebuild can occur within that biophysical limit. Thank you very much. We've come to time. We've come to time. Thanks for taking the time to present to us today. Thank you, committee. Yeah. Right. Now, we move on to Sea Eagle Fishing Limited.
Oh, they're in person. Very good. So I understand we have Samuel and Kristy Penwarden. Penwarden. Good morning. You have 10 minutes. So the time is yours.

Good morning. Thank you for your time today and for the opportunity to speak with you. My name is Kristy Penwarden. This is my brother, Samuel. Together we run our family fishing business. We're independent, operating primarily in the scampi fishery. Our late father, Neil, was a pioneer in the scampi fishery and established this business in 1990.

However, he was involved in fishing from the 1970s in the US and Australia. We currently operate two vessels, one of which has been in our family since 1989. In fact, I actually refer to her as my younger sister. Our other vessel was a purpose-built vessel specifically for fishing scampi, and was commissioned in 2019 and was a demonstration of our commitment to the industry.

Working our family business, picking up where Dad left off is a privilege, and we take great pride in what we do. Dad loved the ocean. He was a fisher, a surfer, a diver- Excuse me... and shared that love and respect of the sea with us. I actually don't have many childhood memories or family memories that don't involve fishing, both recreational and commercial.

It's actually all, well, I've ever known, Sam's ever known, since Dad was fishing before I was born, Sam was born. He's my younger brother. But I do remember as a child, spending a lot of time in the shipyards when the boat was in dry dock for refit, as in those early days, Dad did a lot of the work himself. As such, I genuinely feel like the fabric of our family is interwoven with our business.

It's actually something our children, we have five between us, they often talk about picking up one day and taking on. Dad was not only a pioneer in the scampi fishery, he was also a pioneer in talking about the health and sustainability of this fishery well before anyone else was, and both Samuel and I have grown up with this ethos.

We know how lucky we are to be the stewards of our business, and it's important to us that we can balance operating a successful business operation that supports many local businesses throughout New Zealand with ensuring the sustainability that our sector is managed. And I'd like to hand over to Sam, who will talk specifically about the submission.

Thank you for the opportunity to present today and contribute to the committee's consideration of the bill. Following on from my sister's introduction, I'm the director of our family's fishing operations. I had a separate career as a professional structural design engineer before returning home in 2010 to assist Dad. Like my father, I'm an avid recreational fisherman, surfer, and diver. I've always had a great love for the ocean.

I traveled to be in person as the amendment to a fundamental and good bill like the Fisheries Act does not happen often. The importance of getting this right is paramount. We're in a world of advanced technology, science that the original authors from government, science, and industry could not have anticipated when this bill was originally written.

These proposed amendments will allow us to use the whole toolbox for dynamic and reactive fisheries and marine ecosystem management. To oversimplify the engineering talk, this is essential maintenance on the working machine that is the Fisheries Act. We support the Amendments Bill in principle.

This maintenance will involve the modernization of the Act to reflect better data and monitoring, adapt to a more complex environmental and social expectations, meet the needs for more responsive management tools. However, we emphasize that fisheries systems are complex. Some proposals will need refinement.

Technical issues must be addressed to ensure intended outcomes are achieved. We have submitted a comprehensive submission to you, but time restraints do not allow me to go into it totally.I've been involved in many plenaries and read countless science reviews, both in our scampi business and as a director of the Deep Water Group.

And all the time, it has been apparent that our current structure is rich in data and science but restricted in regulatory bandwidth. This restricts the quality of the information available to meet the needs of the marine ecosystem. We support clarifying the role of the total allowable catch. Too often, management of our fisheries comes down to the TACC and the fisheries management areas. These are regulatory tools, not management tools.

Our current science, whilst robust, is expensive, time-consuming, and mostly three or four years too late. It's equivalent of picking out the All Blacks for the World Cup final from the Bledisloe Cup two years ago. The proposed amendments will use the new science methods and management tools to reduce the information feedback loop.

The introduction of tools like time management, spatial management, and gear-based measures will better meet the needs of the marine ecosystem. There's a lot of noise about the reliance of catch per unit effort being the only measure. It's one of just many tools we would have at our disposal that need to be applied appropriately.

One size does not fit all. We support the amendments enabling the recognition of non-regulatory sustainability measures. The minister needs to have the scope to consider and give appropriate weight to non-regulatory measures. This is not a new concept. Industry has long implemented measures such as ACE shelving, catch spreading, and spatial size limits to support sustainability. These tools are already part of how fisheries are actively managed in practice.

However, the Amendment Bill, as it stands, needs more attention to this area, as we currently risk legal ambiguity. The current drafts risk being more restrictive than the status quo. The strength of a non-regulatory measure is its agility to react to defined indicators and be adjust required and responsibly.

We support creating tools for multi-layered catch decisions. The marine ecosystem does not know of calendars and arbitrary human dates. At best, it dances to its own seasonal tune. Anything we do needs to be flexible and multi-year, and in most cases, specific to the species and the location.

We need to use all the tools we have to be prepared to respond to the fishery if required. There will be times when the best laid plans don't work or Mother Nature just has other ideas. We support the protection of onboard camera footage. The cameras are a regulatory tool, not a public surveillance mechanism, regardless of how they are funded.

The back deck of a fishing boat is a personal space as much as it's a workspace. The potential for footage to be taken out of context and individuals just trying to do their job being vilified in this day and age of cyberbullying is only just one reason. Imagine if you are driving your car on a public road and your car telemetry and car footage was available for the public digestion and review and judgment.

The public needs to have faith in the regulatory body to do its job effectively. The investment we know the fishers quota holders have is significant and long-term. We operate in generational timelines, not yearly nor monthly. We fundamentally never work on short-term gain, long-term loss principles.

The public noise in the amendment that there will be no fish left in the ocean, excuse me, that as the pun, this does not hold water. From a pure economic approach, a commercial fisher will never destroy the most valuable natural resource, thus destroying the asset value on his vessel or her vessel and quota.

I still struggle to see how people are saying it will exclude iwi. Iwi have about a 40% share in New Zealand fisheries. Mm. They will not and cannot be left out of the argument. Mm. There's this notion that New Zealand public will be excluded from the discussion. And granted, I'm an engineer, I'm not a solicitor, but I just don't see how using better science and management tools will do this.

Thank you for your time and the opportunity to speak to you. Thank you very much. We're going to questions with Rachel first. Thank you, Chair. Thank you very much for your submission. I've asked this question of a lot of people and it's around the ACE carry forward. Mm-hmm. We've heard some examples from rock lobster where it would've been useful. Have you had that same situation in your industry?

I can't speak for other fisheries, but for scampi, ACE carry forward in the bill- Yeah... it says up to 100%. But from when I've read it, there are a lot of checks and balances and very specific to the species. Scampi, I would need to look at a scientific review, and you would have to present me with a very robust scientific study to say, "Yes, we can take the carry forward above what we have." But there are times when you can't harvest your ACE, weather mostly, that you can't do it. But- Mm. It's good for humans... it's not a one size fits all.

Mm. You need the science to back it up, and you can't just pull a number from thin air. Yeah. Thank you. Yeah. I was just interested in some of the comments you made around CPUE and other factors that go towards calculating catch and what have you. Could you give the committee a bit of a insight into what other factors are used?

That's a good question. Thanks. Like I said, there are a lot of tools you need to use. CPUEFor fisheries that's mature like scampi, is a good, quick indicator of what you're seeing. It's not the only indicator, because it can deceive you. It's subject to increase in fisheries by vessels improving technology. But we're quite evolved and matured, so we don't have those giant leaps anymore.

But it also gets skewed by weather, ability for fleets to range, location, and temperature bycatch. So again, you got to take that one tool and say, yes, this is an indicator, but you've got to look at everything else in the picture. It's a very complicated thing.

It's not simple, which is why you have to have your rules. You need to consider your management. Whereas if you just take CPUE and TACC, it is literally like using a cannon to kill a mosquito. You basically need to approach it more like a surgeon specific to each operation. Yeah. Because it has been raised then with the increased technology, it's easier, I suppose you could say, to- It's a barometer.

Yeah. Okay. Well, you've come to time. Thank you very much for your submission. We really appreciate it. Thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you. Right. We're going to move on to, um, Patu Productions, and we've got Isaac Te Reina, if that's how you pronounce it, uh, to submit on their behalf.

Thank you.

Isaac, are you there? Can you hear us? Hi. I can. Hi. Yes. Hi, Isaac. Is your camera on? Ah, there we are. There we are. Hey, thank you for taking the time to submit today to the committee. You have 10 minutes. Time is yours. Fire away. Kia ora tatou, tena tatou katoa, morena.

Ko au te moana raukawakawa. Ko te moana raukawakawa ko au. Ko Isaac Te Reina tene. I'm speaking to you today as a recreational fisherman, keen diver, and someone who deeply loves our kaimoana, and a media professional who knows a thing or two about storytelling.

And the story being presented from this bill is incredibly alarming and dangerous to our kaimoana. So I'm providing my truth on what I believe, what story is being told from this government's fisheries bill. The government says this bill is about improving efficiency and responsiveness in fisheries management, but what it's really doing is weakening environmental protections that move us further away from sustainable fishing at a time when our oceans are already under immense pressure from commercial fishers.

We have fished these waters for thousands of years, our diet sustained by plentiful amounts of fish and sea life. Aotearoa has been a place of nourishment for our people of this whenua. Our parents and kaumatua are still able to tell us stories of plentiful koura, pāua everywhere, and fish in abundance along our coastlines.

These times are now gone, and that abundance has declined dramatically within a single lifetime. Today, the next generations of fishermen and divers are growing up with scarcity instead. We see kina barrens, depleted reefs, overfished areas, and stricter rules on everyday people trying to gather kai for their whānau.

Meanwhile, large commercial operators continue taking obscene volumes from our oceans, exporting north of 250,000 tons of fish annually, while we here at home consume only 26,000 tons. The average cost of all our fish is exported overseas for less than $6 per kg. Every day, New Zealanders are paying exorbitant prices, often above $25 per kg at supermarkets, for seafood caught off our own coastlines.

This makes absolutely no sense. We should be ch- um, we should be, uh, cherishing... We should, sorry, we should be charging more for our fish to overseas buyers and less for our people at home, especially when it becomes harder and harder to catch a feed. The science and the common sense are simple. When industrial fishing removes too much from deep ocean, the entire food chain is affected.

Less baitfish means less food for larger species. The ecosystem becomes stressed from the bottom up, and us recreational fishers near the shore are left competing for the scraps of what remains after these high-tech commercial vessels have already swept through. Technology on commercial vessels is becoming more advanced every year.

Fish are easier to locate and catch at scale than ever before. That should mean stronger safeguards, not weaker ones. It seems this government is ignoring the science and research that put these protections in place in the first place. One of the most concerning parts of this bill is how it attempts to reduce transparency around onboard cameras.We're told this is about protecting proprietary commercial information.

Interesting, when this also blocks access and hides the evidence of any bycatch of our threatened and endangered species, the list of which is growing. Case after case of our fish species being increasingly threatened. We fought hard for these cameras to be installed in the first place to reveal overfishing, undersized catch, dumping, and et cetera.

But multiple different commercial entities have been fined over one million dollars for breaching these regulations, and that just isn't enough. Because commercial companies have incredible financial infrastructure, they just treat fines like they are business expenses and carry on. This bill makes it easier for them to continue to act illegally at the expense of our fish and our people.

Utter ludicrous. At a time when many coastal species are already declining, reducing accountability is the wrong direction. We should be here talking about how we are protecting our fish, not just here defending common sense, sustainable practices. These safeguards were put in place to avoid ecosystem collapse. Our oceans should be treated as a taonga, something to protect and pass on to future generations, not simply a resource to maximize profit from at the expense of our stocks declining.

I'm not against commercial fishing, just to be clear. It has an important place in our economy and in feeding people globally. Our fish are clearly of major value to the world. But supplying overseas buyers cannot come at the expense of sustainable public New Zealand access to kai moana and the long-term health of our own oceans.

My worst fear is that future generations will only inherit emptier coastlines and even higher prices for fish at the shop. Our children and our mokopuna deserve the opportunity to experience the same connections to our moana that previous generations once had. This bill does not protect our valuable, fragile ocean ecosystem. It is disgraceful.

This is our ocean, these are our coastlines, and we all have a responsibility to protect them for the generations to come. So thank you for this opportunity to stand up for our kai moana and our oceans. On behalf of recreational fishers, divers, and whanau who enjoy catching their own kai moana, we reject this bill completely. Kia ora.

Kia ora. Thank you for your submission, Isaac. We're going to questions now. We have Steve Abel first. Kia ora, Isaac. Thank you very much for your powerful submission. What's your observations of the health of the fishery and your actions as a fisher yourself?

New Zealand exports about fish. Yeah, there's all kind of information you can find online that is supplied and is credible by government. So yeah, it's quite alarming when you just read these figures and just try and piece together the pieces of what's happening at commercial level.

I think we've got a Ms- Kia ora Beth. Kia ora. Welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee. Thank you. You have 20 minutes for your submission, so fire away. Kia ora. Ngā mihi ana ki a koutou e te whakarongo mai. Kia ora koutou. It's great to see you. I've been looking forward to speaking to you all week, and it's great to be here.

Sure you have. I've enjoyed watching some of the other presenters. So, yeah, it's great to see I'm among such great company. My name is Beth Rose, and I'm the executive officer for the Hauraki Gulf Forum. Tikapa moana te moananui a Toi. Thank you for inviting me. I'm stoked to be presenting on behalf of the forum.

I'm going to take our submission as read in the most part because you have our words there. So I just wanted to provide a bit of context as to why we were compelled to make a submission to the Fisheries Amendment Bill 2026. So from here on in, I will refer to the Hauraki Gulf Forum as the forum or us or we, depending.

I am a bit of a storyteller, so I'm going to take you on a bit of a haerenga, a bit of a journey around Tikapa moana te moananui a Toi, just to explain why we do not support the bill. So let's start with the invitation. The invitation to present today happened to land with me in possibly one of the most relevant contexts, as I was on top of Hirakimata Aotea, Great Barrier Island about to descend the maunga, having spent a couple of days living in the clouds and among the burrows of the tākokatā, black petrel.

I have a very diverse role, you'll see. This manu has gone from the west coast of the South Island and other places, and so it is an endangered bird. Its population has shrunk to just here. And by here, I mean the highest maunga on Aotea. So it is a tonga species for Ngāti Rehua - Ngāti Wai ki Aotea, and there is another smaller population on the neighboring motu of Hauturu, Little Barrier Island. So therefore, it is also a tonga species for Ngāti Manuhiri as well. It's an endemic manu nowhere else, and the chicks fledge from the nests here to begin

their migration to South America. They won't touch land again until they return to within possibly meters of their nest. That is, if they return, because the odds are stacked against them. This means they spend a good proportion of their lives at sea, and they utterly depend on healthy oceans.

When adults feed their chicks, they are doing so from Tikapa moana te moananui a Toi. This whole scenario is emblematic of the wildlife living within Hauraki Gulf. Long distance travelers living among permanent residents, many endemic species relying on the food and shelter provided by this ecosystem. It is part of what makes the Hauraki Gulf Marine Park an internationally significant environment.

Everything is connected, and the tākokatā has a contribution here too, bringing nutrients from the moana up to the ngāhere and keeping the soil and invertebrates healthy, and the forests. They're an example of one species that exists within a much larger food web.

Everything has its part to play. But as we are seeing in the moana, when we disrupt the balance, such as taking too much of one thing out, ecosystems can fail, and we are seeing that in our oceans through overfishing. We cannot manage fisheries in isolation from the ecosystems that sustain them. So why was I among the burrows, collecting stories for the state of our gulf?

The forum is required to produce the State of the Gulf every three years, and I would just note it is a State of the Gulf year this year, and that is coming up on us very soon. It provides the most comprehensive long-term assessment of environmental change in the Marine ParkAnd I'm going to come to that a little bit later, right after I've provided a little bit more context about our act. So in the year 2000, Parliament decided that the Hauraki Gulf Tikapa Moana Te Moana-nui-a-Toi is a nationally significant marine

environment that warrants a distinct and integrated management framework due to its ecological vulnerability, cultural importance, and cumulative pressures. Parliament gave effect to this recognition through the Hauraki Gulf Marine Park Act 2000. It is Aotearoa, New Zealand's first marine park, and it was established to recognize and provide for the historic cultural and spiritual relationship of tangata whenua with the Hauraki Gulf Marine Park, including its islands, shorelines, and marine ecosystems. It is 1.2 million hectares, the largest marine park in

Aotearoa. The Hauraki Gulf Marine Park Act explicitly acknowledges the Hauraki Gulf's national significance and sets out in sections seven and eight environmental and cultural values that must be recognized for. These provisions emphasize the need for coordinated ecosystem-based management across land and sea, reflecting both the complexity of the Hauraki Gulf system and the interconnected nature of the pressures acting upon it, such as fishing.

So that's a bit of a reminder about the legislation underpinning the park. Here's a bit about the forum. The Hauraki Gulf Forum was established to promote the integrated management and long-term protection of the Hauraki Gulf, its islands, and its catchments. It brings together 21 members, including representation from the Minister for Fisheries, the Minister for Conservation, and the Minister for Maori Affairs, Te Puni Kōkiri. It is also made up of members from Tāmaki Makaurau and Waikato regions, including six tangata whenua members, five members of Auckland

Council, members from Aotearoa Great Barrier, a member from Waiheke Local Board, from Waikato Regional Council, Waikato District Council, Hauraki District Council, Matamata-Piako District Council, and Thames-Coromandel District Council. And I do just want to acknowledge our members, not least our co-chairs, Nicola Rata McDonald and Councillor Warren Ma, for their interest, energy, and enthusiasm for this work. The forum has an intentionally collaborative governance structure for an area where management responsibilities,

legislative frameworks, and environmental impacts span multiple jurisdictions, and it upholds Te Tiriti o Waitangi. Now that I've had the opportunity to provide this context, I would just like to note as a broader point, we are finding that in various reforms and amendments that we have seen recently, existing legislation such as our act and the laws and promises within it are either being ignored, bypassed, or forgotten about.

An example of that is also the Natural Environment Planning bills, which seek to replace the RMA, which of course, is a piece of legislation that the Hauraki Gulf Marine Park Act mentions considerably. There is no mention of our act in these bills. There are echoes of the same treatment of silent bypass when it comes to the Fisheries Amendment bill and its lack of consideration for ecosystem-based management, as acknowledged in Sea Change in the Marine Spatial Plan for the Hauraki Gulf.

In the rush to create efficiencies, there is a high risk that chaos is created as the main result. Then there is an issue of bias. While we are delighted to welcome the Hauraki Gulf Tikapa Moana Marine Protection Act last year, I would like to note that even there in that legislation to protect the marine environment, concessions were made for the benefit of commercial fishing through the allowance of ring net fishing in high protection areas.

Neither were there any tools created as part of that legislation to increase protection. It was limited to just 6% high protection, which of course is nowhere near the 30% that is recommended as an international goal. So how is it that legislation can be developed to protect the marine environment that then shows sympathy to commercial fishing within high protection areas, but then when we're looking at the Fisheries Amendment bill, no sympathy or acknowledgment is shown towards the broader marine environment, indeed, the ecosystems on which these fisheries

depend? All this is to say that we are looking for more consistency, consideration, and common sense across the development of legislation, please. Putting these disappointments aside, the Hauraki Gulf Tikapa Moana Marine Protection Act was acknowledgment from Parliament that protection was needed due to the ongoing decline of the Hauraki Gulf.

The enactment demonstrates existing management settings have not been sufficient to halt degradation, and that coordinated place-based intervention is required to enable ecological recovery. So as Parliament has already determinedThe Hauraki Gulf needs ecosystem-focused management and environmental protection.
Sorry, pausing.

And must be central to decision-making within the gulf. And we do not believe that the Fisheries Amendment Bill has been assembled with that in mind, or other obligations within the Fisheries Act 1996 that require fishery settings to provide for the need of future generations. I would like to move on to some of the evidence that the forum provides through its reporting that might help give some basis and direction for our submission, and that might hopefully be considered in the development of legislation in the future, whether that is in support of fisheries or to help

regenerate Te Kupumuana-Te Moananui-a-Toi more generally. So the state of the gulf. What these reports are showing consistently over time is a pattern of cumulative decline. We're seeing the loss of marine habitats, reduced water quality, and a significant reduction in the abundance and diversity of marine life.

Once plentiful species such as tipa, scallops, koura, crayfish, and kutai, mussels, have either collapsed or are under significant pressure, and the overall productivity of the system has diminished. Importantly, the report makes it clear that this is not the result of a single pressure, but the combined effects of fishing, land-based impacts, climate change, and historical management decisions that have not accounted for the system as a whole.

What is particularly striking is that the decline is systemic. It's not confined to one part of the gulf or one sector. The State of the Gulf reporting shows that when ecosystems tip out of balance, impacts are felt across the entire network, from habitats and species through to fisheries and community experience.

And this reinforces a central point, really, that the forum has consistently been making. The health of fish stocks cannot be separated from the health of the ecosystems that support them. In that context, the findings of the State of the Gulf align closely with the economic analysis undertaken by the New Zealand Institute of Economic Research in 2023, which demonstrates that the gulf is a multi-billion dollar natural asset, providing more than 5 billion in value annually, much of which depends on functioning, healthy ecosystems. The implication is

clear. Continued ecological decline is an environmental issue, not just an environmental issue, but a growing economic risk, reinforcing the need to place ecosystem health at the center of decision-making. This value extends far beyond traditional economic measures, including cultural, recreational, and ecosystem services. When the ecosystem is degraded, that value is diminished, and the cost of repairs shifts to the communities and the public.

So on the topic of cost, anyone working in the marine environment will be acutely aware of the cost of doing so. It is such an expensive place to work, which is why ecosystem regeneration and repair is so incredibly expensive and time-consuming.

The cost of this, as we've seen in Te Kupumuana, is being picked up currently by philanthropy and communities, repairing the reefs from the damage brought about through the decline of koura, crayfish, and other kina-eating species. This work is happening so that we might see the rimurimu kelp reefs come back to provide the nurseries that our fish need, and that we therefore need for healthy fish stocks. It is hard work, and I would also like to add that a lot of the expense is unaccounted for, given the enormous amount of unpaid work that is happening through kaitiakitanga

and volunteerism. We are so fortunate to have engaged communities and leadership from Mana Whenua. So the restoration of these places is hugely important. We need only to look at the world's first no-take marine reserve at Cape Rodney-Okakari Point, otherwise known as Leigh or Goat Island Marine Reserve, which is now over 50 years old and still one of the very few marine protected areas that we have in this country, to see the important role that healthy ecosystems do play.

They are fish nurseries. Large, productive tarahumi or snapper here, along with koura, crayfish, are helping to grow populations of these species across the gulf. Perhaps then we can start to build some legislation that gets around the thing that we do all agree on, more fish, whether that is fish that we catch for ourselves, fishing that contributes to the economy through sustainable fishing methods. I haven't mentioned anything about bottom trawling here, which I could. And commercial operators of fish that we can see in our waters along coastlines, for

those of us who just like to be in the water admiring our marine environment, or fish that contribute to the biomass that supports a healthy ocean and the role that the ocean plays in absorbing the heatFrom this warming planet of ours. So, whilst we do not support the Fisheries Amendment Bill 2026, we do believe that the Fisheries Act 1996 needs improving, and we would love to see a review of the act because fishing methods, technology, monitoring capability have changed significantly since these legislative settings were

introduced. At the same time, fish stocks and marine ecosystems have experienced growing pressure from overfishing and wider human-induced impacts, including climate change, marine heat waves, pollution, and sedimentation. And just to be clear, given current management settings have led to fisheries closures due to collapsing stocks with regards to quota and TPAP particularly, there's no clearer signal than that to determine that things are not working as they should be.

We support reform, but we do not support this bill. The concern for the forum is that this bill prioritizes flexibility and efficiency without sufficiently strengthening ecosystem considerations, transparency, or long-term sustainability outcomes. It risks reinforcing the very dynamics that have led to decline, and the forum's position is that it's a missed opportunity.

We would prefer to see a more comprehensive review of the Fisheries Act that places ecosystem health at the center of decision-making. This includes strengthening transparency, maintaining strong incentives for sustainable fishing behavior, and aligning with ecosystem-based approaches already being developed, and we're seeing some of that, what the good of that looks like through SeaChange, Tai Tīmu Tai Para, Pare, sorry, and the development of the Hauraki Gulf Fisheries Plan.

These approaches recognize that fisheries outcomes depend on ecosystem health. So I will finish where I began, standing on Haurakimata last week. It was clear that the health of ecosystems depends on decisions far beyond that place. If we do not align fisheries management with ecosystem reality, we are not reducing cost. We are simply going to defer it.

And that cost will ultimately be borne by communities, by the environment, and by future generations. So thank you, and I would just like to acknowledge, as I sort of did at the start, all the people who I have actually seen presenting here earlier in the week. It's been great to hear all of their perspectives as well.

I do feel like I'm in some really great company and there's a lot of shared values out there. So ngā mihi. Ngā mihi. Thanks, Beth. Thank you, Beth. We've got a question from Rachel Boyack. Thank you, Chair. Thank you, Beth, and thank you for all the work that you do and that the forum does. I've got a specific question related to your written submission.

So bullet point eight, which is about the ALI decision in 2025. Mm. You've said here that the bill ignores the direction from the court- Mm... and you're concerned about the bill prioritizing industry certainty. I'm just interested in the particular clauses of the bill that you've identified, and if you don't have this information now, it can always come back to us.

But the particular clauses in the bill that you think undermine that court decision, because that's quite an important thing for us to consider. We were reflecting there quite broadly on, it was pointed out last year that the minister needed to take a whole-of-ecosystem approach.

We don't feel like that's coming across. Okay. Yeah, essentially in quite a few aspects. But yeah, it was quite a clear direction. It was a reminder about putting the ecosystem at the heart of a lot of the legislation development, and so yeah, we're just not seeing that being acknowledged here really.
Okay. Thank you. Thank you very much, Beth. Kia ora. Appreciate you taking the time to present to us today. Thank you. Thank you.
